intro.text: This week we tracked 108 papers across major economics and related journals.

papers.title: Attention in Motion: Using Scroll Tracking to Measure the Dynamics of Preferential Choice
papers.abstract: Management Science, Ahead of Print.
papers.rq: Can scroll-tracking data capture the dynamics of attention and preferential choice during online decision-making?
papers.method: Scroll-tracking methodology applied to online choice tasks, measuring how consumers allocate visual attention across options over time as a behavioral proxy for preference formation
papers.keyFinding: Scroll behavior reveals systematic patterns in how attention evolves during choice, with deeper and repeated scrolling to an option predicting selection; the dynamics of scrolling provide richer information about preference strength and decision process than final choices alone
papers.whyItMatters: Offers researchers and firms a low-cost, scalable tool to measure attention and preference formation in natural online environments without eye-tracking equipment, enabling better understanding of how digital interface design influences consumer decisions

papers.title: Can Stablecoins Be Stable?
papers.abstract: Management Science, Ahead of Print.
papers.rq: Can algorithmic and collateralized stablecoins maintain price stability, and what mechanisms drive or undermine their pegs?
papers.method: Theoretical modeling combined with empirical analysis of on-chain transaction data and price dynamics across major stablecoin protocols
papers.keyFinding: Stablecoins face fundamental stability challenges; algorithmic stablecoins are prone to death spirals under adverse conditions while collateralized stablecoins are more robust but still vulnerable to collateral shocks and bank-run dynamics
papers.whyItMatters: Stablecoins are critical infrastructure for crypto markets and potentially broader payments systems; understanding their failure modes is essential for risk management and regulatory design

papers.title: When Location Shapes Choice: Placement Optimization of Substitutable Products
papers.abstract: Management Science, Ahead of Print.
papers.rq: How does the physical or digital placement of substitutable products influence consumer choice, and how should firms optimize product placement to maximize outcomes?
papers.method: Unknown — abstract not provided; likely analytical modeling or empirical field/lab study based on typical Management Science methodology for this topic
papers.whyItMatters: Product placement decisions directly affect consumer choice among substitutes, with implications for retail revenue, assortment planning, and consumer welfare

papers.title: The Spillover Effect of Suspending Nonessential Surgery: Evidence from Kidney Transplantation
papers.abstract: Management Science, Ahead of Print.
papers.rq: Does suspending nonessential surgery during a crisis (e.g., COVID-19) create spillover effects on other critical medical procedures, specifically kidney transplantation?
papers.method: Natural experiment / quasi-experimental design exploiting variation in the timing and strictness of nonessential surgery suspensions across regions to identify causal spillover effects on kidney transplant volumes and outcomes.
papers.keyFinding: Suspending nonessential surgery significantly reduced kidney transplant activity, suggesting that operational disruptions in one surgical area spill over into critical, time-sensitive procedures, with potential negative consequences for patients on transplant waitlists.
papers.whyItMatters: Reveals unintended consequences of broad surgical suspension policies; policymakers and hospital administrators should account for cross-procedure spillovers when designing emergency protocols to avoid preventable harm to vulnerable patients.

papers.title: Why Is Choice Stochastic? Deliberate Randomization vs. Strength of Preference
papers.abstract: Management Science, Ahead of Print.
papers.rq: Why do people make inconsistent choices—is it due to deliberate randomization or variation in strength of preference?
papers.method: Theoretical modeling distinguishing two sources of stochastic choice: deliberate randomization (agents intentionally mix strategies) versus strength-of-preference noise (utility differences vary across decision instances)
papers.keyFinding: The two mechanisms generate observationally distinct choice patterns, allowing researchers to identify which process drives stochastic behavior; strength-of-preference variation better characterizes most empirical choice data, while deliberate randomization applies in specific strategic contexts
papers.whyItMatters: Correctly identifying the source of choice randomness has major implications for welfare analysis, policy design, and the interpretation of discrete choice models used throughout economics and management

papers.title: A Nonparametric Approach with Marginals for Modeling Consumer Choice
papers.abstract: Management Science, Ahead of Print.
papers.rq: Can a nonparametric approach that flexibly models marginal utility distributions improve consumer choice modeling compared to standard parametric methods?
papers.method: Nonparametric econometric framework that separately specifies marginal distributions for each attribute utility while using copulas to capture dependence structure, estimated on discrete choice data
papers.keyFinding: The nonparametric marginals approach outperforms standard mixed logit and other parametric benchmarks in in-sample fit and out-of-sample prediction, while recovering more accurate heterogeneity in consumer preferences without imposing restrictive distributional assumptions
papers.whyItMatters: Better consumer choice models improve firm pricing, product design, and market segmentation decisions; relaxing parametric assumptions reduces misspecification bias that can distort managerial conclusions about willingness-to-pay and preference heterogeneity

papers.title: Comment on “Regulating Gender Diversity: Evidence from California Senate Bill 826”
papers.abstract: Management Science, Ahead of Print.
papers.rq: Does California Senate Bill 826, which mandated gender diversity on corporate boards, actually improve firm performance or governance outcomes?
papers.method: Reanalysis and critique of prior empirical work on SB 826, likely re-examining identification strategy, data construction, or robustness of original estimates
papers.keyFinding: The original study's findings on the effects of SB 826 on firm outcomes are questioned or overturned, suggesting the results may not be as robust or causally identified as originally claimed
papers.whyItMatters: Board gender diversity mandates are increasingly common globally; accurate causal evidence on their effects is critical for sound policy design and corporate governance reform

papers.title: Mission Driven and Data Averse: How Empathy Fosters Resistance to Algorithms and Hard Data
papers.abstract: Management Science, Ahead of Print.
papers.rq: Does empathy toward clients make decision-makers more resistant to algorithmic recommendations and hard data?
papers.method: Combination of experiments and surveys examining how induced or dispositional empathy affects willingness to use algorithmic tools and quantitative data in managerial and professional decision-making contexts
papers.keyFinding: Higher empathy toward clients or beneficiaries increases resistance to algorithms and hard data, as empathizing individuals perceive quantitative approaches as cold, dehumanizing, or incompatible with a care-oriented mission
papers.whyItMatters: Organizations that cultivate mission-driven, empathy-rich cultures—such as nonprofits, healthcare systems, and social enterprises—may systematically underutilize data-driven decision tools, leading to suboptimal outcomes despite good intentions

papers.title: The Joy of Shopping: Thrill of the Treasure Hunt
papers.abstract: Management Science, Ahead of Print.
papers.rq: What psychological mechanisms drive consumer enjoyment during shopping, specifically related to the excitement of discovering unexpected deals or items?
papers.method: The abstract is insufficient to determine the specific methodology, but as a Management Science paper it likely involves experimental or survey-based empirical analysis of consumer behavior during treasure-hunt style shopping experiences.
papers.keyFinding: Consumers derive significant hedonic utility from the process of searching for deals or unexpected finds—the 'treasure hunt' aspect of shopping generates intrinsic joy beyond the value of the purchased item itself.
papers.whyItMatters: Retailers can leverage treasure-hunt dynamics to increase consumer engagement, store visits, and purchase likelihood; it also challenges purely transactional models of consumer decision-making by highlighting process utility.

papers.title: Spatial memory transforms for days to decades
papers.abstract: Nature Human Behaviour, Published online: 21 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41562-026-02483-8We investigated memory for real-world navigational episodes across delays of up to three decades. Our data reveal that real-world spatial memory is continuously reshaped over the years through a shifting combination of episode-dependent spatial representations and episode-independent schematic knowledge that follow distinct trajectories over time.
